meta.subject: Chemistry
meta.scoring_potential_analysis: High. Mechanisms are standard. 'Give Reason' questions on Acidity and Boiling Point are predictable. Name reactions (Reimer-Tiemann, Kolbe, Williamson) are mandatory.

chapter_flow.why_section_matters_exam_wise: Basic literacy. Important only for identifying 'Vinylic' vs 'Allylic' alcohols and IUPAC names of phenols.

core_concepts.definition_or_law_verbatim: The bond angle in alcohols is slightly less than the tetrahedral angle (109.28'). It is due to the repulsion between the unshared electron pairs of oxygen.
core_concepts.concept_clarity_explanation: Oxygen is sp3 hybridized. In phenols, the C-O bond length (136 pm) is slightly less than in methanol due to partial double bond character (resonance) and sp2 hybridised carbon.

cognitive_layering.weak_student_layer: Alcohols have H-bonding -> High Boiling Point.
cognitive_layering.board_ready_layer: Bond angle C-O-H in alcohol is < 109 degrees due to lone pair repulsion. In ether, C-O-C angle is > 109 degrees (111.7) due to steric repulsion of alkyl groups.
cognitive_layering.topper_mastery_layer: Compare Dipole moments: Alcohol vs Phenol vs Ether.

exam_intelligence.why_examiner_tests_this: Tests understanding of VSEPR and Hybridisation consequences.
trap_patterns: Thinking bond angle in ether is also < 109 (It is actually greater due to bulky groups).

diagram_json.common_exam_twist: Explain why bond angle in ether is larger than tetrahedral angle.

rapid_revision_block.ultra_compact_revision: Alcohols: sp3 C, Angle < 109. Phenols: sp2 C, C-O bond short (Resonance). Ethers: Angle > 109 (Steric hindrance). IUPAC: Phenol derivatives named as substituted phenols (e.g., 2-Bromophenol).

chapter_flow.why_section_matters_exam_wise: Conversions rely heavily on these methods. Hydroboration and Cumene process are frequent questions.

core_concepts.definition_or_law_verbatim: Hydroboration-Oxidation: Alkenes react with diborane to form trialkyl boranes which upon oxidation by H2O2 in alkaline medium yield alcohols (Anti-Markovnikov addition of water).
core_concepts.concept_clarity_explanation: From Alkenes: Acid catalyzed hydration (Markovnikov). Hydroboration (Anti-Markovnikov). From Carbonyls: Reduction (Aldehyde -> 1 deg, Ketone -> 2 deg). Grignard: HCHO -> 1 deg, RCHO -> 2 deg, RCOR -> 3 deg.

cognitive_layering.weak_student_layer: Hydroboration = Anti-Markovnikov OH addition. Grignard adds carbons.
cognitive_layering.board_ready_layer: Mechanism of Acid Catalysed Hydration. Cumene -> Phenol + Acetone (Important industrial method).
cognitive_layering.topper_mastery_layer: Why Grignard reagent isn't used with acidic impurities (Protonation destroys Reagent).

exam_intelligence.why_examiner_tests_this: Tests regioselectivity (Mark vs Anti-Mark) and carbon chain ascent (Grignard).
trap_patterns: Asking product of Hydroboration of propene (Propan-1-ol) vs Acid Hydration (Propan-2-ol).
Forgetting Acetone as byproduct in Cumene process.
frequent_misconceptions: Sodium Borohydride (NaBH4) reduces esters (No, it only reduces aldehydes/ketones. LiAlH4 reduces esters).

stepwise_mechanism: Step 1: Protonation of alkene to form carbocation (Electrophilic attack of H3O+).
Step 2: Nucleophilic attack of water on carbocation.
mechanism_block.energy_profile_description: Three steps, Carbocation is intermediate valley.

components_to_label: Arrow from double bond to H+
Arrow from H2O lone pair to C+

rapid_revision_block.ultra_compact_revision: Hydration: Mark. Hydroboration: Anti-Mark. NaBH4 reduces CHO/CO. LiAlH4 reduces CHO/CO/COOH/Ester. Grignard: Methanal -> 1 deg, Other Ald -> 2 deg, Ketone -> 3 deg.
must_remember_points: Phenol from Diazonium salt: Warm with water.

chapter_flow.why_section_matters_exam_wise: Acidity order and Name Reactions (Kolbe, Reimer-Tiemann) are the most repeated questions in Board history.

core_concepts.ncert_alignment_scope: 7.6.1 Reactions involving cleavage of O-H bond
core_concepts.definition_or_law_verbatim: Phenols are more acidic than alcohols because the phenoxide ion is stabilized by resonance, whereas alkoxide ion is destabilized by +I effect of alkyl groups.
core_concepts.concept_clarity_explanation: Acidity Order: Phenol > Water > Primary Alc > Secondary > Tertiary. Electron Withdrawing Groups (NO2) increase acidity (stabilize phenoxide). Electron Donating Groups (CH3) decrease acidity.

cognitive_layering.weak_student_layer: Phenol is acidic (turns blue litmus red). Alcohol is neutral. NO2 makes Phenol stronger acid.
cognitive_layering.board_ready_layer: Compare acidity of o-nitrophenol vs p-nitrophenol (Intramolecular H-bond in ortho reduces acidity slightly/affects volatility, but p-isomer is usually more acidic due to better solvation? Actually p-nitrophenol is slightly more acidic).
cognitive_layering.topper_mastery_layer: pKa values: Phenol (10), Ethanol (15.9). Explain why o-nitrophenol is steam volatile (Intramolecular H-bond).

exam_intelligence.why_examiner_tests_this: Tests application of Inductive and Resonance effects.
trap_patterns: Thinking Alcohols are more acidic than water (False, Alkyl group +I destabilizes alkoxide).
Comparing o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol boiling points (Ortho is lower due to chelation).
frequent_misconceptions: Phenol reacts with NaHCO3 (No, it is not acidic enough to release CO2. Carboxylic acids do).

core_concepts.definition_or_law_verbatim: Lucas Test: Distinguishes primary, secondary and tertiary alcohols using Lucas Reagent (Conc HCl + ZnCl2).
core_concepts.concept_clarity_explanation: Esterification: Alcohol + Acid <-> Ester + Water (conc H2SO4 removes water to shift equilibrium). Lucas Test: 3 deg (Turbidity immediate), 2 deg (Turbidity in 5 min), 1 deg (No turbidity at room temp).

cognitive_layering.weak_student_layer: Alcohol + Acid -> Fruity smell (Ester). Lucas: 3-fast, 2-slow, 1-no reaction.
cognitive_layering.board_ready_layer: Esterification mechanism (Nucleophilic attack of alcohol on carbonyl). Aspirin preparation (Salicylic acid + Acetic anhydride).
cognitive_layering.topper_mastery_layer: Role of Pyridine in reaction with acyl chloride (Neutralizes HCl formed to drive reaction forward).

exam_intelligence.why_examiner_tests_this: Practical application and distinguishing functional groups.
trap_patterns: Forgetting that Lucas test requires ZnCl2 (except for 3 deg which reacts with HCl alone due to stable carbocation).
Aspirin structure (Acetyl group on Oxygen, not Ring).

stepwise_mechanism: Step 1: Protonation of Carbonyl Oxygen of Acid.
Step 2: Nucleophilic attack of Alcohol Oxygen on Carbonyl Carbon.
Step 4: Loss of Water and Deprotonation.

core_concepts.ncert_alignment_scope: 7.6.2 Reactions involving cleavage of C-O bond
core_concepts.definition_or_law_verbatim: Dehydration: Alcohols lose water to form alkenes. Order: 3 > 2 > 1. Oxidation: 1 deg -> Aldehyde -> Acid. 2 deg -> Ketone. 3 deg -> No reaction (or C-C cleavage under drastic conditions).
core_concepts.concept_clarity_explanation: Dehydration Mechanism (H2SO4, 443K) is E1 (via Carbocation). Oxidation with mild agents (PCC, CrO3) stops at Aldehyde. Strong agents (KMnO4) go to Acid. Heated Cu: Dehydrogenation.

cognitive_layering.weak_student_layer: Ethanol (443K) -> Ethene. Ethanol (413K) -> Ether. PCC makes Aldehyde.
cognitive_layering.board_ready_layer: Mechanism of Dehydration of Ethanol. Heated Cu test (3 deg alcohol gives Alkene, not carbonyl!).
cognitive_layering.topper_mastery_layer: Difference between PCC and Jones Reagent.

trap_patterns: 3 deg alcohol with Cu/573K gives Alkene (Elimination) instead of oxidation.
frequent_misconceptions: PCC oxidizes to Carboxylic acid (No, stops at Aldehyde).

stepwise_mechanism: Step 2: Loss of water to form Carbocation (Slow, RDS).
Step 3: Loss of proton to form Alkene (Fast).

core_concepts.definition_or_law_verbatim: Reimer-Tiemann: Phenol + CHCl3 + NaOH -> Salicylaldehyde. Kolbe's: Phenol + NaOH + CO2 -> Salicylic Acid.
core_concepts.concept_clarity_explanation: Electrophilic Substitution: OH is ortho/para directing and activating. Nitration (Dil HNO3 -> Mono; Conc HNO3 -> Picric Acid). Bromination (CS2 -> Mono; Bromine water -> 2,4,6-Tribromophenol).

cognitive_layering.weak_student_layer: Kolbe = Acid (Salicylic). Reimer-Tiemann = Aldehyde. Bromine water = White ppt.
cognitive_layering.board_ready_layer: Mechanism of Reimer-Tiemann involves carbene (:CCl2) intermediate. Oxidation of phenol gives Benzoquinone.
cognitive_layering.topper_mastery_layer: Why does Bromination in water give poly-substitution? (Phenol ionizes to Phenoxide, which is highly activating).

trap_patterns: Confusing reagents of Kolbe (CO2) and Reimer-Tiemann (CHCl3).
Bromination conditions: CS2 (low polarity) gives Monobromophenol. Water (high polarity) gives Tribromophenol.

diagram_json.common_exam_twist: Draw structure of product when phenol is treated with Zinc dust (Benzene).

rapid_revision_block.ultra_compact_revision: Lucas Test: 3>2>1 (HCl/ZnCl2). Dehydration: 443K (Alkene), 413K (Ether). Oxidation: PCC (Aldehyde), KMnO4 (Acid). Phenol: Kolbe (Acid), R-T (Aldehyde), Bromine water (Tribromo), Zinc dust (Benzene).
must_remember_points: Picric acid is 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (Prepared using conc HNO3).

chapter_flow.why_section_matters_exam_wise: Williamson Synthesis is the primary method. Cleavage by HI is a logical puzzle tested often.

core_concepts.definition_or_law_verbatim: Williamson Synthesis: Reaction of alkyl halide with sodium alkoxide. Follows SN2 mechanism. Best yield when alkyl halide is primary.
core_concepts.concept_clarity_explanation: If alkyl halide is 3 deg, elimination happens (alkene formed) because alkoxide is a strong base. To make t-butyl methyl ether, use Methyl Halide + Sodium t-Butoxide.

cognitive_layering.weak_student_layer: R-X + R-ONa -> R-O-R. Halide must be small (primary).
cognitive_layering.board_ready_layer: Dehydration of alcohols (413K) gives ethers (SN2). Only works for primary alcohols. 2/3 deg give alkenes.
cognitive_layering.topper_mastery_layer: Why aryl halides don't undergo Williamson synthesis? (Bond strength). Use Phenoxide + Alkyl Halide.

trap_patterns: Proposing t-Butyl chloride + Sodium Methoxide to make ether (Gives Isobutylene). Correct is t-Butoxide + Methyl Chloride.

stepwise_mechanism: 2. SN2 attack of second alcohol molecule on protonated alcohol (removal of water).

core_concepts.definition_or_law_verbatim: Reaction with HI: R-O-R' + HI -> R-I + R'-OH (if R is smaller). If one group is tertiary, halide goes to tertiary carbon (SN1). If aromatic, phenol is always formed.
core_concepts.concept_clarity_explanation: General rule (SN2): I- attacks less hindered carbon. Exception (SN1): If stable carbocation (3 deg, benzyl, allyl) can form, I- goes there. Anisole: O-C(phenyl) bond has partial double bond character, so it doesn't break. I- attacks methyl group.

cognitive_layering.weak_student_layer: Ether + HI -> Alcohol + Iodide. Small group gets Iodine.
cognitive_layering.board_ready_layer: Tertiary ether -> Tertiary Iodide (SN1). Anisole -> Phenol + Methyl Iodide.
cognitive_layering.topper_mastery_layer: Reaction with excess HI converts the alcohol formed also into iodide (unless it's phenol).

trap_patterns: Cleavage of Anisole: Students often put Iodine on benzene (impossible) or make Iodobenzene + Methanol (Wrong).
Benzyl ethyl ether + HI -> Benzyl Iodide + Ethanol (Benzyl cation stability).

stepwise_mechanism: Step 2 (SN2): I- attacks less hindered carbon from back, displacing alcohol.
Step 2 (SN1 - if 3 deg): Bond breaks to form stable carbocation, then I- attacks.

components_to_label: Attack of I- on Methyl carbon

rapid_revision_block.ultra_compact_revision: Williamson: Primary Halide + Alkoxide. Cleavage by HI: SN2 (I- to smaller group). SN1 (I- to 3 deg/Benzyl). Anisole + HI -> Phenol + CH3I. Electrophilic Sub on Anisole: o/p directing.
must_remember_points: Friedel Crafts on Anisole doesn't require Lewis Acid? No, it requires AlCl3.
Nitration of Anisole gives o/p nitroanisole.

high_probability_areas_2026: Mechanism of Acid Catalysed Hydration of Ethene
examples_that_must_not_be_confused: Reaction of Anisole with HI (Phenol+CH3I) vs Benzyl Ether with HI (Benzyl Iodide)
chapter_master_control.last_minute_revision_strategy: Draw the 3 mechanisms (Hydration, Dehydration, HI cleavage). Memorize the reagents for PCC, Jones, Lucas, Reimer-Tiemann. Review the acidity order.

formula_trigger_engine.trigger_situation: Comparing Acidity of Phenols and Alcohols
formula_trigger_engine.why_this_formula: Phenoxide ion is resonance stabilized; Alkoxide is not. Electron Withdrawing Groups (NO2) stabilize phenoxide further.
formula_trigger_engine.common_wrong_formula: Thinking Alcohols are stronger acids because O-H bond looks similar.
formula_trigger_engine.unit_or_sign_trap: pKa values are inverse to Acidity. Lower pKa = Stronger Acid.

formula_trigger_engine.trigger_situation: Reactivity of Alcohols with Lucas Reagent (Conc. HCl + ZnCl2)
formula_trigger_engine.why_this_formula: Reaction proceeds via carbocation intermediate. 3° carbocation is most stable.
formula_trigger_engine.unit_or_sign_trap: Lucas test is for distinguishing alcohols. 3° turns turbid immediately. 2° in 5 mins. 1° only on heating.

formula_trigger_engine.why_this_formula: Alkoxide attacks alkyl halide. Best results with 1° Alkyl Halide.
formula_trigger_engine.common_wrong_formula: Using 3° Alkyl Halide with 1° Alkoxide (Results in Elimination/Alkene).
formula_trigger_engine.unit_or_sign_trap: To make t-butyl ethyl ether: Use Sodium t-butoxide + Ethyl bromide. NOT t-butyl bromide + Sodium ethoxide.

numerical_pattern_lab.recognition_signal: Ether + HI (1 eq or excess). Predict products.
step_sequence: 2. Case A: If one group is 3° (Tertiary) -> SN1. I- attacks 3° Carbon (Stable Carbocation).
3. Case B: If both are 1°/2° -> SN2. I- attacks LESS hindered Carbon.
4. Case C: If Aromatic (Anisole) -> Ph-O bond implies Partial Double Bond. Bond NEVER breaks. I- attacks Alkyl group.
numerical_pattern_lab.hidden_exam_trap: Reaction of Anisole (Ph-O-CH3) with HI gives Phenol + CH3I. NEVER Iodobenzene + Methanol.

numerical_pattern_lab.problem_archetype: Dehydration of Alcohols: Temperature Dependent Products
numerical_pattern_lab.hidden_exam_trap: Writing Ether as product when temp is 443 K.

mechanism_classification_trainer.decision_logic: CHCl3 + OH- -> :CCl2 (Neutral electrophile). Attacks electron-rich ring.
mechanism_classification_trainer.common_confusion: Thinking the electrophile is CHCl2+ or CHO+ directly.

mechanism_classification_trainer.decision_logic: Acid: Carbocation intermediate (2° stable). Hydroboration: Concerted cyclic transition state (sterics dictate B goes to terminal C).
mechanism_classification_trainer.common_confusion: Forgetting that Hydroboration-Oxidation involves SYN addition (H and OH added to same face).

reaction_pathway_mapper.exam_conversion_pattern: Most important industrial method. By-product (Acetone) is valuable.

possible_routes: 1. NaOH, CO2 (Kolbe's Reaction) -> Salicylic Acid (ortho-hydroxybenzoic acid).
reaction_pathway_mapper.exam_conversion_pattern: Phenol -> Salicylic Acid -> Aspirin. Watch out for acetylation of OH group, not COOH.

inorganic_comparison_arena.correct_insight: Ortho: Intramolecular H-bonding (Chelation) -> Low BP, Volatile. Para: Intermolecular H-bonding (Association) -> High BP, Non-volatile.
inorganic_comparison_arena.examiner_trap: Assuming Para is volatile due to symmetry. Volatility depends on lack of association.

inorganic_comparison_arena.correct_insight: BP: Ethanol >> Ether (H-bonding in alcohol). Solubility: Both soluble (Ether forms H-bonds WITH water, but not with itself).
inorganic_comparison_arena.examiner_trap: Thinking Ethers are insoluble in water like alkanes. Small ethers are soluble due to oxygen lone pairs.

exception_bank.general_trend: Electrophilic substitution in Phenol requires Lewis Acid catalyst.
exception_bank.exception: Bromination of Phenol takes place without FeBr3.
exception_bank.why_exception_occurs: OH group is highly activating. Polarization of Br2 molecule occurs by the ring itself.
R group is electron donating (+I), destabilizing alkoxide. (Exception: Methanol is slightly more acidic than water).
exception_bank.exam_question_style: Compare acidity of Ethanol and Water.

diagram_activation_zone.diagram_type: Mechanism of Acid Catalysed Hydration of Ethene
diagram_activation_zone.what_student_must_instantly_recognize: Step 1: Protonation to form Carbocation. Step 2: Nucleophilic attack of Water. Step 3: Deprotonation.
diagram_activation_zone.exam_twist_pattern: Asking for the rate determining step (Formation of Carbocation).

diagram_activation_zone.diagram_type: Resonance Structures of Phenoxide vs Phenol
diagram_activation_zone.what_student_must_instantly_recognize: Phenoxide has charge separation? No, delocalization of negative charge. Phenol involves charge separation (+ on O, - on Ring).
diagram_activation_zone.exam_twist_pattern: Why is Phenol acidic? Phenoxide resonance is more stable (no charge separation) than Phenol resonance (charge separation).

top_5_numerical_traps: Acidity order: p-nitrophenol > m-nitrophenol. (Meso/Resonance effects vs Inductive only at meta).
Boiling point: Alcohol > Ether > Hydrocarbon (Mass comparable).
Preparation of t-butyl methyl ether: Only one route works (Me-X + t-BuONa).
Reaction of t-butyl ether with HI: Forms t-butyl iodide (SN1).
top_5_conversion_patterns: Ethanol -> Acetone (Oxidation -> Grignard -> Oxidation... or Cu/573K route)
top_3_derivation_flash_prompts: Mechanism of Dehydration of Ethanol to Ethene.
Explain why phenols are more acidic than alcohols using resonance.

visual_elements.visual_title: Mechanism of Acid Catalysed Dehydration of Ethanol to Ethene
visual_elements.what_the_visual_represents: Stepwise conversion of alcohol to alkene at 443 K involving carbocation intermediate.

stepwise_flow: Step 1: Protonation of alcohol (Fast/Reversible). Form alkyloxonium ion.
Step 2: Loss of water to form Carbocation (Slow/RDS).
Step 3: Elimination of a proton from beta-carbon to form Ethene.
mechanism_steps.energy_profile_description: Highest activation energy barrier at Step 2.

exam_question_patterns.how_examiner_frames_it: Write the mechanism of acid dehydration of ethanol to yield ethene.
exam_question_patterns.student_must_do: Write all 3 steps clearly with curved arrows. Mark the Slow step.

exam_question_patterns.how_examiner_frames_it: What is the intermediate formed in the dehydration of alcohols?

graph_interpretation_traps: Confusing this with the mechanism of Ether formation (occurs at 413 K).
Forgetting to show the lone pairs on Oxygen during protonation.
labeling_mistakes_students_make: Omitting the H+ catalyst regeneration in the final step.

visual_elements.visual_title: Acidity of Phenols: Resonance Stabilization of Phenoxide Ion
visual_elements.what_the_visual_represents: Comparison of stability between Phenol and Phenoxide ion to explain acidity.

important_labels: Negative charge delocalization in Phenoxide (more stable)
key_points_to_mark: Negative charge at Ortho and Para positions
No charge separation in Phenoxide structures

stepwise_flow: Negative charge on Oxygen overlaps with benzene ring.
Delocalization stabilizes the ion, pushing equilibrium to the right.

exam_question_patterns.how_examiner_frames_it: Explain why phenol is more acidic than ethanol using resonance structures.
exam_question_patterns.student_must_do: Draw resonance of Phenoxide. State that Ethoxide has no resonance and the alkyl group (+I) destabilizes the negative charge.

graph_interpretation_traps: Thinking Phenol is acidic because of resonance in Phenol itself (It is actually the stability of the Conjugate Base that matters more).
Comparing Phenol acidity with Carboxylic acids (Phenols are weaker, don't react with NaHCO3).
labeling_mistakes_students_make: Putting negative charge on Meta positions.

visual_elements.visual_title: Mechanism of Formation of Ethoxyethane (Ether) from Ethanol
visual_elements.what_the_visual_represents: Nucleophilic substitution (SN2) of protonated alcohol by another alcohol molecule.

stepwise_flow: Step 2: Nucleophilic attack of second Ethanol on protonated Ethanol (SN2). Water leaves.
mechanism_steps.stereochemical_outcome: Inversion (though not observable in symmetric primary alcohols)

exam_question_patterns.how_examiner_frames_it: Why is the preparation of ethers by acid dehydration of alcohols not suitable for secondary and tertiary alcohols?
exam_question_patterns.student_must_do: Explain that for 2°/3°, Elimination (Alkene formation) competes and dominates over Substitution (Ether formation) due to stability of carbocation.

graph_interpretation_traps: Writing the mechanism for 443 K (Alkene) instead of 413 K (Ether).
Trying to apply this mechanism to prepare unsymmetrical ethers (results in mixture).
labeling_mistakes_students_make: Forgetting that the reaction is continuous (catalytic H+).

visual_elements.what_the_visual_represents: The limitation of Williamson synthesis when using tertiary alkyl halides.

important_labels: Case A: CH3Br + t-Butoxide -> t-Butyl Methyl Ether (SN2)
Case B: t-Butyl Bromide + Methoxide -> Isobutylene (Elimination)
key_points_to_mark: Alkoxide acts as Base in Case B
Alkoxide acts as Nucleophile in Case A

exam_question_patterns.how_examiner_frames_it: Predict the products when Sodium tert-butoxide reacts with Methyl bromide vs Sodium methoxide reacting with tert-Butyl bromide.
exam_question_patterns.student_must_do: Identify SN2 for primary halide (Ether product) and E2 for tertiary halide (Alkene product).

graph_interpretation_traps: Thinking the reaction works equally well for all halides.
Confusing the role of the alkoxide (Nucleophile vs Base).
labeling_mistakes_students_make: Drawing ether product for the tertiary halide reaction.

visual_elements.what_the_visual_represents: Industrial preparation involving oxidation and hydrolysis.

exam_question_patterns.how_examiner_frames_it: Name the starting material used for the industrial preparation of phenol that yields a useful byproduct.

graph_interpretation_traps: Forgetting the structure of Cumene Hydroperoxide.
labeling_mistakes_students_make: Drawing propyl benzene instead of isopropyl benzene (Cumene).

must_draw_diagrams: Mechanism of hydration of ethene to ethanol
Mechanism of dehydration of ethanol to ethene (443 K)
Mechanism of dehydration of ethanol to ether (413 K)
must_interpret_graphs: Boiling point comparison: Alcohols vs Alkanes vs Ethers (Alcohols highest due to H-bonding)
must_explain_mechanisms: Reaction of Lucas Reagent with 1°, 2°, 3° alcohols
top_5_visual_traps: Mixing up the temperature conditions for Ether (413K) vs Alkene (443K) formation.
Applying Williamson synthesis with Tertiary Halide hoping for Ether (You get Alkene).
Drawing resonance for Phenol to explain acidity instead of Phenoxide (Conjugate base stability is the key).
Assuming Phenols react with NaHCO3 (They don't, they are weaker than carbonic acid).
Forgetting the carbocation rearrangement (Hydride/Methyl shift) in dehydration of secondary alcohols.
